title: String Functions
description: Common string manipulation functions: searching, splitting, formatting, and pattern matching

concepts.title: Search and Replace

sqlServer.code:
DECLARE @text NVARCHAR(200) = N'Contact us at support@example.com for help.';

-- CHARINDEX: find position of substring (1-based, 0 if not found)
SELECT CHARINDEX('@', @text) AS at_position;              -- 22

-- PATINDEX: find position using SQL wildcard pattern
SELECT PATINDEX('%[a-z]@%', @text) AS pattern_position;  -- 21

-- REPLACE: substitute all occurrences of a substring
SELECT REPLACE(@text, 'support@example.com', 'help@company.com') AS replaced_text;
-- Result: 'Contact us at help@company.com for help.'

-- STUFF: delete length characters at start position, then insert replacement
-- STUFF(string, start, length, replacement)
SELECT STUFF(
  @text,
  CHARINDEX('support@example.com', @text),
  LEN('support@example.com'),
  'help@company.com'
) AS stuffed_text;
-- Result: 'Contact us at help@company.com for help.'
sqlServer.description: SQL Server provides CHARINDEX for finding substring positions, PATINDEX for pattern-based searching using SQL wildcard syntax (%, _, [a-z]), REPLACE for straightforward substitution, and STUFF for positional deletion-plus-insertion. CHARINDEX returns 0 when the substring is not found.
notes: CHARINDEX is case-insensitive by default (depends on collation)
PATINDEX uses SQL wildcard patterns (%, _, [charlist]), not POSIX regex
STUFF is useful when you know the exact position and length to replace
REPLACE replaces all occurrences; there is no built-in single-occurrence replace

postgres.code:
DO $$
DECLARE
  v_text TEXT := 'Contact us at support@example.com for help.';
BEGIN
  -- POSITION / STRPOS: find position of substring (1-based, 0 if not found)
  RAISE NOTICE 'at_position: %', POSITION('@' IN v_text);     -- 22
  RAISE NOTICE 'strpos result: %', STRPOS(v_text, '@');        -- 22 (alias)

  -- REPLACE: substitute all occurrences (identical to SQL Server)
  RAISE NOTICE 'replaced: %',
    REPLACE(v_text, 'support@example.com', 'help@company.com');

  -- REGEXP_REPLACE: replace using POSIX regular expression
  RAISE NOTICE 'regexp_replace: %',
    REGEXP_REPLACE(v_text, '[a-z]+@[a-z]+\.[a-z]+', 'help@company.com');

  -- OVERLAY: SQL-standard positional replacement (equivalent to STUFF)
  -- OVERLAY(string PLACING replacement FROM start FOR length)
  RAISE NOTICE 'overlay: %',
    OVERLAY(v_text
      PLACING 'help@company.com'
      FROM    STRPOS(v_text, 'support@example.com')
      FOR     LENGTH('support@example.com'));
END;
$$;

-- Inline SELECT examples
SELECT
  STRPOS('hello world', 'world')                            AS position_result,  -- 7
  REPLACE('foo bar foo', 'foo', 'baz')                      AS replace_result,   -- 'baz bar baz'
  REGEXP_REPLACE('price: 9.99', '[0-9\.]+', '10.00')       AS regex_result,     -- 'price: 10.00'
  OVERLAY('hello world' PLACING 'PostgreSQL' FROM 7 FOR 5)  AS overlay_result;  -- 'hello PostgreSQL'
postgres.description: PostgreSQL offers POSITION(substr IN str) or STRPOS(str, substr) for locating substrings, REPLACE for simple substitution, REGEXP_REPLACE for POSIX-regex-powered replacement, and OVERLAY as the SQL-standard positional replacement equivalent to T-SQL STUFF. REGEXP_REPLACE is considerably more powerful than PATINDEX because it uses full POSIX regex syntax.
keyDifferences: PATINDEX uses SQL wildcard patterns (%, _); PostgreSQL REGEXP_REPLACE uses full POSIX regular expressions
PostgreSQL has no STUFF function; use OVERLAY(string PLACING ... FROM ... FOR ...) instead
POSITION uses SQL standard syntax POSITION(substr IN str); STRPOS(str, substr) is the functional alias
REGEXP_REPLACE accepts an optional flags argument ('g' for global, 'i' for case-insensitive)
notes: STRPOS and POSITION are interchangeable; STRPOS matches the argument order of CHARINDEX
Use REGEXP_REPLACE with flag 'g' to replace all occurrences when using a regex pattern
OVERLAY is part of the SQL standard; it is less commonly seen but fully supported

concepts.keyDifference: PATINDEX uses SQL Server wildcard patterns (%, _, []); PostgreSQL uses REGEXP_REPLACE with full POSIX regex syntax. T-SQL STUFF becomes PostgreSQL OVERLAY for positional replacement.

concepts.title: String Splitting

sqlServer.description: STRING_SPLIT (introduced in SQL Server 2016) splits a delimited string into a single-column table result named 'value'. It can be used in FROM clauses and JOINs like any table. OPENJSON parses JSON arrays into rowsets and is the idiomatic way to shred JSON arrays into rows.
notes: STRING_SPLIT does not guarantee order; use WITH (ENABLE_ORDINAL) in SQL Server 2022+ for positional ordinal
The single-character delimiter limitation was lifted in SQL Server 2022
OPENJSON can also parse JSON objects into key/value rows with typed columns using WITH clause

postgres.description: PostgreSQL splits strings by combining string_to_array with UNNEST, or using REGEXP_SPLIT_TO_TABLE for pattern-based splitting. WITH ORDINALITY provides the positional index that SQL Server 2022's ENABLE_ORDINAL offers. For JSON arrays, jsonb_array_elements_text is the idiomatic equivalent of OPENJSON.
keyDifferences: PostgreSQL uses UNNEST(STRING_TO_ARRAY(...)) where SQL Server uses STRING_SPLIT()
PostgreSQL's REGEXP_SPLIT_TO_TABLE accepts full POSIX regex delimiters; SQL Server STRING_SPLIT only accepts a single character
WITH ORDINALITY provides row position natively; no special compatibility level required
jsonb_array_elements_text replaces OPENJSON for JSON array shredding
notes: string_to_array(str, delimiter, null_string) has a third argument to treat a specific value as NULL
UNNEST can expand multiple arrays in parallel in PostgreSQL 9.4+
regexp_split_to_array returns TEXT[] which can then be indexed or passed to other array functions

concepts.keyDifference: SQL Server uses STRING_SPLIT() as a table-valued function; PostgreSQL uses UNNEST(STRING_TO_ARRAY()) or REGEXP_SPLIT_TO_TABLE(). PostgreSQL's approach supports regex delimiters and WITH ORDINALITY for preserving row order.

concepts.title: String Aggregation

sqlServer.code:
-- Setup: sample employee data
CREATE TABLE #employees (
  department NVARCHAR(50),
  name       NVARCHAR(100)
);
INSERT INTO #employees VALUES
  ('Engineering', 'Alice'), ('Engineering', 'Bob'),
  ('Sales', 'Carol'), ('Sales', 'Dave'), ('Sales', 'Eve');

-- STRING_AGG (SQL Server 2017+): concatenate values with a delimiter
SELECT
  department,
  STRING_AGG(name, ', ') WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY name) AS members
FROM #employees
GROUP BY department;
-- Engineering | Alice, Bob
-- Sales       | Carol, Dave, Eve

-- FOR XML PATH (legacy, SQL Server 2005+): pre-2017 workaround
SELECT
  department,
  STUFF(
    (SELECT ', ' + name
     FROM #employees e2
     WHERE e2.department = e.department
     ORDER BY name
     FOR XML PATH('')),
    1, 2, ''
  ) AS members
FROM #employees e
GROUP BY department;

DROP TABLE #employees;
sqlServer.description: STRING_AGG (SQL Server 2017+) aggregates values into a delimited string, with WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY ...) controlling sort order. The legacy FOR XML PATH('') technique used STUFF to strip the leading delimiter and is still encountered in older codebases.
notes: STRING_AGG requires SQL Server 2017 (compatibility level 140+)
WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY ...) is mandatory to guarantee consistent ordering
FOR XML PATH encodes special XML characters (&, <, >) — use STRING_AGG for new code
NULL values are automatically skipped by STRING_AGG

postgres.code:
-- Setup: sample employee data
CREATE TEMP TABLE employees_agg (
  department TEXT,
  name       TEXT
);
INSERT INTO employees_agg VALUES
  ('Engineering', 'Alice'), ('Engineering', 'Bob'),
  ('Sales', 'Carol'), ('Sales', 'Dave'), ('Sales', 'Eve');

-- STRING_AGG: concatenate values with a delimiter (available since PostgreSQL 9.0)
SELECT
  department,
  string_agg(name, ', ' ORDER BY name) AS members
FROM employees_agg
GROUP BY department;
-- Engineering | Alice, Bob
-- Sales       | Carol, Dave, Eve

-- ARRAY_AGG + ARRAY_TO_STRING: alternative producing same result
SELECT
  department,
  array_to_string(array_agg(name ORDER BY name), ', ') AS members
FROM employees_agg
GROUP BY department;

-- Filter with FILTER clause (PostgreSQL extension)
SELECT
  string_agg(name, ', ' ORDER BY name)
    FILTER (WHERE department = 'Engineering') AS eng_members
FROM employees_agg;

DROP TABLE employees_agg;
postgres.description: PostgreSQL's string_agg is nearly identical to SQL Server's STRING_AGG, with ORDER BY placed directly inside the aggregate call rather than in a separate WITHIN GROUP clause. It has been available since PostgreSQL 9.0, predating SQL Server 2017's introduction. The FILTER clause provides inline conditional aggregation.
keyDifferences: ORDER BY is placed inside string_agg(expr, delim ORDER BY ...) rather than in a separate WITHIN GROUP clause
PostgreSQL string_agg has been available since version 9.0; SQL Server STRING_AGG requires 2017+
No FOR XML PATH workaround is needed; string_agg is available in all supported PostgreSQL versions
The FILTER (WHERE ...) clause allows conditional aggregation inline without a subquery
notes: NULL values are automatically excluded from string_agg, same as SQL Server
ARRAY_AGG produces a PostgreSQL array; use ARRAY_TO_STRING to convert to a delimited string
string_agg can be used as a window function with OVER() for non-grouping aggregation

concepts.keyDifference: Both platforms have STRING_AGG with nearly identical syntax, but SQL Server places ORDER BY in a separate WITHIN GROUP clause while PostgreSQL places it inside the aggregate call. PostgreSQL has had string_agg since version 9.0; SQL Server added it in 2017.

concepts.title: Formatting and Padding

sqlServer.code:
DECLARE @num  INT      = 42;
DECLARE @dt   DATETIME = '2026-03-15 09:05:03';
DECLARE @str  NVARCHAR(20) = '   hello   ';

-- FORMAT: zero-pad an integer to 6 digits using .NET format strings
SELECT FORMAT(@num, 'D6') AS zero_padded;          -- '000042'

-- FORMAT: format a date/time as a string
SELECT FORMAT(@dt, 'yyyy-MM-dd')   AS date_str;    -- '2026-03-15'
SELECT FORMAT(@dt, 'HH:mm:ss')     AS time_str;    -- '09:05:03'

-- REPLICATE + RIGHT: manual zero-padding (faster than FORMAT for bulk ops)
SELECT RIGHT(REPLICATE('0', 6) + CAST(@num AS NVARCHAR(10)), 6) AS replicate_padded;
-- '000042'

-- LEFT / RIGHT: extract fixed-width substrings from the left or right
SELECT LEFT('Hello World', 5)  AS left_5;   -- 'Hello'
SELECT RIGHT('Hello World', 5) AS right_5;  -- 'World'

-- LTRIM / RTRIM: remove leading/trailing spaces
SELECT LTRIM(@str) AS left_trimmed;         -- 'hello   '
SELECT RTRIM(@str) AS right_trimmed;        -- '   hello'
SELECT LTRIM(RTRIM(@str)) AS both_trimmed;  -- 'hello'
sqlServer.description: SQL Server's FORMAT function uses .NET composite format strings for numbers and dates, making it flexible but slower than dedicated functions for high-volume operations. REPLICATE builds a repeated-character string. LTRIM/RTRIM trim only one side; combining them trims both sides. SQL Server 2022 adds TRIM with character specification.
notes: FORMAT has higher overhead than CONVERT or CAST; avoid it in row-by-row processing of large datasets
REPLICATE + RIGHT is the traditional high-performance zero-padding pattern
SQL Server 2017+ TRIM(chars FROM string) trims both sides and can remove specific characters
LEFT and RIGHT return NULL if the string is NULL, and clamp silently if length exceeds string length

postgres.code:
-- LPAD / RPAD: pad a string on the left or right to a given width
SELECT LPAD(42::TEXT, 6, '0')  AS zero_padded;    -- '000042'
SELECT RPAD('hi', 10, '-')     AS right_padded;   -- 'hi--------'

-- TO_CHAR: format numbers and dates using PostgreSQL format templates
SELECT TO_CHAR(42, 'FM000000')              AS to_char_num;   -- '000042'
SELECT TO_CHAR(CURRENT_DATE, 'YYYY-MM-DD') AS date_str;      -- '2026-03-15'
SELECT TO_CHAR(NOW(), 'HH24:MI:SS')        AS time_str;      -- '09:05:03'
SELECT TO_CHAR(9999.99, 'FM$999,999.00')   AS money_str;     -- '$9,999.99'

-- LEFT / RIGHT: identical to SQL Server
SELECT LEFT('Hello World', 5)  AS left_5;   -- 'Hello'
SELECT RIGHT('Hello World', 5) AS right_5;  -- 'World'

-- TRIM / LTRIM / RTRIM: remove whitespace (or specific characters)
SELECT TRIM('   hello   ')                  AS both_trimmed;  -- 'hello'
SELECT LTRIM('   hello   ')                 AS left_trimmed;  -- 'hello   '
SELECT RTRIM('   hello   ')                 AS right_trimmed; -- '   hello'
SELECT TRIM(BOTH 'x' FROM 'xxhelloxx')      AS char_trimmed;  -- 'hello'

-- REPEAT: equivalent to SQL Server REPLICATE
SELECT REPEAT('0', 6)  AS repeated_zeros;  -- '000000'
postgres.description: PostgreSQL uses LPAD/RPAD for direct padding, TO_CHAR for formatted number and date strings using PostgreSQL-specific format templates (not .NET format strings), and TRIM for whitespace removal. TRIM supports LEADING, TRAILING, and BOTH with optional character lists. REPEAT replaces SQL Server's REPLICATE.
keyDifferences: SQL Server FORMAT uses .NET format strings ('D6', 'yyyy-MM-dd'); PostgreSQL TO_CHAR uses its own template syntax ('FM000000', 'YYYY-MM-DD')
PostgreSQL has native LPAD/RPAD functions; SQL Server achieves the same with REPLICATE + RIGHT
PostgreSQL TRIM(BOTH 'x' FROM str) can trim arbitrary characters, not just spaces
REPEAT is PostgreSQL's equivalent of SQL Server REPLICATE
notes: The FM prefix in TO_CHAR format strings suppresses leading/trailing spaces and zero-padding in output
LPAD/RPAD cast the first argument to TEXT implicitly, so numeric types must be cast explicitly
TO_CHAR is locale-aware for number formatting; use 'FM' prefix for consistent output across locales

concepts.keyDifference: SQL Server uses FORMAT with .NET-style format strings and REPLICATE for padding; PostgreSQL uses TO_CHAR with its own template syntax and dedicated LPAD/RPAD functions. PostgreSQL TRIM supports arbitrary character removal natively.
